nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving tributaries, small streams, and flooded forest margins of the central Brazilian Amazon basin (Rio Purus drainage and environs); soft, acidic, often tannin-stained blackwater streams over fine sand and leaf litter

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential for barbel health)
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for shelter
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
smooth-edged caves or PVC pipe hiding spots

diet.notes: Benthic omnivore and opportunistic scavenger. Accepts sinking pellets, wafers, and catfish tablets as staples. Relishes frozen and live foods: bloodworm, tubifex, daphnia, brine shrimp, and micro-worms. Supplement with blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber). Forages through substrate for uneaten food; should not be relied upon solely as a “tank cleaner” — dedicated feeding is necessary.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (neon, cardinal, ember, rummy-nose)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) with adequate space
avoidWith: boisterous fast-moving fish that outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Corydoras schwartzi; standard reference photo showing characteristic lateral stripe and head mask pattern.
Wikimedia Commons image of Corydoras schwartzi in aquarium; verify current license on file page before use.

summary: Schwartz’s Catfish is a peaceful, shoaling armoured catfish from the central Brazilian Amazon (Rio Purus drainage), recognisable by its bold lateral stripe and dark head mask. An easy-care beginner species best kept in groups of 6+ over fine sand. Often confused with C. agassizii (fw-fish-247) and C. loxozonus; pending reclassification to Hoplisoma schwartzi (Dias & Britto, 2024).
careNotes:
Schwartz’s Catfish (Corydoras schwartzi) is a small, hardy armoured catfish endemic to the central Brazilian Amazon, most reliably documented from the Rio Purus drainage. A group of at least 6 individuals is strongly recommended — smaller groups show stress, reduced activity, and increased susceptibility to disease. Fine sand substrate is essential: Corydoras forage by sifting sand through their gills, and coarse gravel can damage delicate barbels, leading to bacterial infection. Keep the substrate clean with regular siphoning, as uneaten food and detritus degrade water quality rapidly in the bottom layer.

Water quality should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15), reflecting the blackwater streams of the Purus system. Tannin-stained water achieved through driftwood and leaf litter is ideal. Temperature range is 22–26°C; avoid elevated temperatures, as Corydoras are more heat-sensitive than many tropical fish. Corydoras species should never be kept in salt-dosed tanks — they are highly salt-sensitive. Copper-based medications are also harmful; always use copper-free treatments.

Schwartz’s Cory is frequently confused with Agassiz’s Corydoras (C. agassizii, fw-fish-247) and the Loxozonus Cory (C. loxozonus) due to overlapping spot-and-stripe patterns and variable individual markings. Reliable identification requires close examination of snout length, the precise arrangement of the lateral stripe, and geographic provenance. C. schwartzi typically shows a more distinct dark mask through the eye and a cleaner, unbroken lateral stripe compared to C. agassizii.

Handle with care: like all Corydoras, C. schwartzi possesses locking pectoral and dorsal spines that can deliver a mildly venomous prick — use a soft net and avoid bare hands during netting. Do not keep with salt-dosed community tanks or with copper-based treatments.

A 2024 molecular phylogenetic revision by Dias & Britto proposes reclassification of C. schwartzi into the resurrected genus Hoplisoma as H. schwartzi; this revision is pending wider adoption and the name Corydoras schwartzi remains the accepted trade name.
breedingNotes: Corydoras schwartzi can be bred in captivity with moderate effort. A temperature drop of 2–3°C simulating the onset of Amazonian cool-season rains is a reliable spawning trigger, combined with increased water changes using slightly cooler, softer water. A 2:1 male-to-female ratio is typical. Eggs are adhesive and deposited on plant leaves, tank glass, or fine-leaved plants such as Java moss. Adults may consume eggs; remove eggs or parents promptly after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 4–5 days at 24°C; fry are free-swimming within a further 2–3 days. First foods: infusoria, micro-worms, and finely powdered fry food.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras schwartzi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rio Purus drainage, Brazil), water parameters (temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 18–179 ppm), adult size (males to ~5 cm, females slightly larger to ~6 cm), diet, substrate requirements, breeding notes, and confusion species (C. agassizii, C. loxozonus).

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras schwartzi
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), native range (Brazil, Rio Purus system), adult size (to 5.9 cm TL), temperature range (22–26°C), diet classification (omnivore, benthic), and venomous spine notation. Also surfaced reference to Dias & Britto 2024 Hoplisoma reclassification in nomenclature records.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-focused husbandry guide for the Corydoras genus. Confirmed minimum group size of 6, fine sand substrate requirement, feeding protocols (sinking foods, live/frozen supplements), salt sensitivity, and copper medication caution applicable to C. schwartzi. Venomous spine handling caution noted for genus.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras: the complete guide
additional.notes: Covered Corydoras identification challenges, specifically the confusion between C. schwartzi, C. agassizii, and C. loxozonus due to variable stripe-and-spot patterns. Noted softwater Brazilian biotope preference and importance of barbel health via fine substrate. Breeding trigger (cool water change) corroborated.

additional.name: Dias, A.C. & Britto, M.R. (2024) — Hoplisoma reclass (molecular phylogeny reference)
additional.notes: Reference for the 2024 Dias & Britto molecular phylogenetic revision proposing reclassification of C. schwartzi to Hoplisoma schwartzi. Name Corydoras schwartzi remains in active trade use; Hoplisoma schwartzi noted as pending synonym. Surfaced via FishBase nomenclature records.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; spawning triggered by cool water changes (2–3°C drop) and increased water flow. Eggs deposited on glass or plants; hatch in ~4–5 days at 24°C. Remove eggs post-spawning to prevent predation by parents. Fry raised on infusoria and micro-worms.
